subject: Physics
title: Refraction and Snell's Law
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Refraction and Snell's Law.

hook.text: Stick a pencil in water and it looks bent. That's refraction—light changes speed and direction.

buildup.text: Light travels slower in glass and water than in air. When it crosses the boundary, it bends.

discovery.text: Snell's law gives the exact angle: the ratio of sines equals the ratio of speeds in each material.

twist.text: Willebrord Snell discovered the law in 1621 but never published it. René Descartes published it later.

climax.text: Refraction makes eyeglasses, cameras, microscopes, and telescopes possible.

punchline.text: Light slows down and bends. That simple fact built all of optics.

quiz.question: Why does light bend when entering water from air?
options: It changes speed at the boundary between the two materials
Water molecules push the light sideways
Gravity pulls light toward the water
